query: pharmacokinetics of AZD3759 blood-brain barrier penetration

retrieved_docs.title: Zafirlukast -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Zafirlukast demonstrates an extensive plasma protein binding rate of 99%. The drug primarily binds to albumin. A volume of distribution of 70 L indicates a moderate level of tissue distribution. In preclinical studies, zafirlukast displayed minimal penetration across the blood-brain barrier. Metabolism: Zafirlukast undergoes primary hepatic metabolism mediated by CYP2C9 enzymes. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Trastuzumab's mean apparent volume of distribution is ~44 mL/kg, similar to its serum volume. Whether trastuzumab traverses the blood-brain barrier or penetrates the cerebrospinal fluid remains unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Sacubitril-Valsartan -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: The mean apparent volumes of distribution of valsartan and sacubitril are 75 and 103 L, respectively. Sacubitril, LBQ657, and valsartan have high plasma protein binding (94% to 97%). LBQ657 crosses the blood-brain barrier to a small extent (0.28%). Metabolism: Sacubitril is converted to LBQ657 by esterases. Valsartan is minimally metabolized (20%), and a hydroxyl metabolite is present in plasma at low concentrations (< 10%).

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: At a steady state, the volume of distribution of deucravacitinib was 140 L. Eighty-two percent to 90% of deucravacitinib was protein-bound, and its blood-to-plasma concentration ratio was 1.26. Patient body weight, age, gender, race, and ethnicity did not have a clinically significant effect on systemic exposure to deucravacitinib. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Brain Barrier -- Other Issues -- Non-Invasive or Miscellaneous Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibition of efflux transporters that impede drug delivery

retrieved_docs.title: Almotriptan -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Almotriptan narrows the brain's blood vessels, reduces cerebral blood flow, and reduces the transmission of pain signals to the brain. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Elimination: The terminal half-life of deucravacitinib is 10 hours. [3] The renal clearance of deucravacitinib ranges from 27 to 54 mL/min. After administration of a single radiolabeled deucravacitinib dose, about 13% and 26% were excreted unchanged in urine and feces, respectively. Approximately 6% and 12% of this single dose were recovered as BMT-153261 in urine and feces, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Nintedanib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: When administered with food,  Nintedanib (BIBF 1120) reaches peak plasma concentrations within 2 to 4 hours. The absolute bioavailability is 4.7% in healthy individuals. The presence of transporters and significant first-pass metabolism reduce absorption and bioavailability. Following food intake, nintedanib exposure increases by roughly 20% compared to fasting conditions, with delayed absorption. The pharmacokinetics of nintedanib are linear, with steady-state plasma levels attained within 1 week.

retrieved_docs.title: Olmesartan -- Mechanism of Action -- Absorption
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal absorption of an oral pill The bioavailability is about 26% and unaltered with the consumption of food. Distribution [4] The volume of distribution is approximately 17 L as it has a high affinity for plasma proteins. It does not enter red blood cells; animal studies also show poor blood-brain barrier penetration.

retrieved_docs.title: Ondansetron -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution : Ondansetron and its metabolites are extensively distributed in tissues. The apparent volume of distribution(Vd) at a steady state is approximately 1.8 L/kg. Ondansetron crosses the blood-brain barrier to a lower extent, with the CSF concentration only about 10%-15% of the plasma concentration in human volunteers. Adenosine binding cassette subfamily 1(ABCB1) is a drug efflux transporter known to transport ondansetron across the blood-brain barrier, thus limiting its accumulation in the CNS. In a patient with decreased activity of ABCB1, the concentration of ondansetron in the brain increases and improves efficacy. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: Peglitocase has a half-life of about 14 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Following oral administration of the extended-release formulation of upadacitinib, absorption occurs within 2 to 4 hours. Distribution: Upadacitinib exhibits approximately 52% plasma protein binding. Metabolism: Metabolism of upadacitinib primarily occurs through CYP3A4, with a minor contribution from CYP2D6. [16] [17] Excretion: Upadacitinib is excreted unchanged in feces (38%) and urine (24%). The mean terminal elimination half-life of upadacitinib ranges from 8 to 14 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Disulfiram -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Disulfiram's high lipid solubility facilitates its widespread distribution into fatty tissue throughout the body, and its ability to cross the blood-brain barrier increases its distribution into the central nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: Deucravacitinib undergoes N-demethylation mediated by cytochrome P-450 (CYP) 1A2 to form the metabolite BMT-153261, which demonstrates comparable pharmacological activity to the parent drug. The circulating BMT-153261 accounts for approximately 20% of the systemic exposure of the total drug-related components. Deucravacitinib is also metabolized by CYP2B6, CYP2D6, carboxylesterase (CES) 2, and uridine glucuronyl transferase (UGT) 1A9. [3] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Angiotensin Receptor Blockers and HCTZ Combination Drugs -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Valsartan and olmesartan bind strongly to plasma proteins, primarily albumin, which significantly reduces their volume of distribution. The volume of distribution for both drugs is 17 L, implying these medications are not distributed extensively throughout the body. Angiotensin receptor blockers such as telmisartan are highly bound to plasma proteins (>99.5%) regardless of dose amount. Telmisartan's distribution volume is 500 L. [28] Irbesartan is 90% bound to plasma proteins (primarily albumin and α-1-acid glycoprotein), and its volume of distribution ranges from 53 to 93 L. Candesartan is highly bound to plasma proteins and has been shown to cross the blood-brain barrier in animal models; its volume of distribution is 0.13 L/kg. Losartan is 99.7% bound to plasma proteins, giving it poor tissue distribution. [26] Eprosartan at any concentration binds strongly to plasma proteins. HCTZ is 40% to 68% bound to albumin in the serum and extensively distributed throughout the body. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Primidone -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: The volume of distribution (Vd) is 0.8 L/kg, while the plasma concentrations of primidone are between 5 and 12 μg/mL; it is rapidly distributed in the tissues and shows a partial serum protein binding (about 35%); the therapeutic range is between 23 to 55 mg/mL. Primidone crosses the blood-brain and placental barriers and is also distributed in breast milk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2528
retrieved_docs.content: Flibanserin is chemically linked to the benzimidazoly scaffold, indicating that its structure is derived from or mapped from benzimidazoly. In addition, flibanserin’s pharmacologic activity is mediated through inhibition of P‑glycoprotein, as it functions as a P‑glycoprotein inhibitor. This dual information—structural mapping to benzimidazoly and a mechanism of action involving P‑glycoprotein inhibition—provides insight into flibanserin’s pharmacokinetic behavior, potential drug‑drug interactions, and guides its clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinflammatory products for vaginal administration are classified within the ATC category “Other gynecologicals”. This therapeutic class also comprises the drugs flibanserin and bremelanotide, each identified as members of the “Other gynecologicals” ATC group. Flibanserin is chemically linked to the benzimidazoly scaffold, indicating that its structure is derived from or related to benzimidazoly. In addition, flibanserin’s mechanism of action involves inhibition of P‑glycoprotein, a transporter that can affect drug absorption and distribution. Together, these relationships define flibanserin and bremelanotide as agents belonging to the “Other gynecologicals” category, with flibanserin further characterized by its benzimidazoly origin and P‑glycoprotein‑inhibiting activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1116
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers affect the pharmacokinetics of several drugs, including the benzodiazepine klobazam, by altering its metabolic pathway through their mechanism of action. These inducers also have a contraindicated mechanism of action with flibanserin, meaning that co‑administration can diminish flibanserin’s therapeutic effect and should be avoided. Chemically, flibanserin is derived from a benzimidazoly scaffold, linking its structure to this heterocyclic core. In addition to its metabolic considerations, flibanserin acts as a P‑glycoprotein inhibitor, influencing drug transport and potentially increasing the exposure of other substrates of this efflux pump. Recognizing the interplay among Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers, klobazam, flibanserin, benzimidazoly chemistry, and P‑glycoprotein inhibition is essential for optimizing drug therapy and preventing adverse interactions.

retrieved_docs.content: Cilansetron is linked to both karbazoly and pyridiny through a “mapped from” relationship, indicating that its chemical structure or pharmacologic profile is derived from or shares key features with these entities. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1056
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers can modify the pharmacologic activity of several central nervous system agents, including the benzodiazepine‑like drug klobazam and the antiepileptic topiramat, by influencing their mechanisms of action. In addition, Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers have a contraindicated mechanism of action with flibanserin, indicating that concurrent use may lead to adverse or ineffective outcomes. The chemical scaffold benzimidazoly is mapped from flibanserin, highlighting a structural relationship between these compounds. P‑Glycoprotein inhibitors also affect drug action by altering the mechanism of action of flibanserin and the antiepileptic zonisamid, reflecting their role in modulating drug transport and efficacy. Understanding how Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers and P‑Glycoprotein inhibitors interact with klobazam, topiramat, flibanserin, and zonisamid is essential for optimizing therapeutic regimens and preventing harmful drug‑drug interactions.
